metadata.description: Luganda grammar rules for the 8 word classes (parts of speech) with examples
metadata.verification_notes: wc_004 (Adjective) was flagged. Corrected by: (1) fixing the Luganda name from 'Nakongeza Linnya' to 'Nakongeza Erinnya', (2) expanding adjective examples with clearer prefix agreement illustrations, (3) adding a concrete note explaining why agreement changes happen, (4) clearing the flag. wc_005 (Adverb) had a misleading example — 'yaggwa nnyo' corrected to 'yaggwa wansi' (he fell down) and nnyo handled separately. wc_006 (Conjunction): 'nga' clarified as it means 'like/as/while' not simply 'and'. All 8 classes verified. Question words section verified clean.

introduction: Luganda words are organized into 8 classes, similar to the 8 parts of speech in English. Every word belongs to one of these classes. Understanding word classes helps you build correct sentences in Luganda.

word_classes.description: Doing words — words that describe an action or a state.
word_classes.how_to_identify: If the word describes something someone does or experiences, it is a verb.

word_classes.description: Names of people, places, or things — words that name something.
word_classes.how_to_identify: If the word is the name of a person, a place, or an object, it is a noun. Nouns can be proper (a specific name) or common (a general name).

examples.english: Moses (proper noun — a specific person's name)

sentence_example.english: Moses cooks food. (Musa and emmere are both nouns.)

word_classes.important_note: In Luganda, nouns belong to noun classes. The class a noun belongs to changes how adjectives and verbs are formed around it. This is one of the most important grammar features of Luganda.

word_classes.description: Words used instead of a noun — they replace the name of a person or thing to avoid repetition.
word_classes.how_to_identify: If the word replaces a name (like he, she, it, you, we, they), it is a pronoun.

word_classes.description: Words that describe or give more information about a noun or pronoun — they tell you what kind, what size, or what quality something is.
word_classes.how_to_identify: If the word tells you more about a noun (big, clean, young, bad), it is an adjective.

examples.note: omunene = big/large. The prefix omu- matches the noun class of omusajja (man).
omulungi = beautiful/good. The prefix omu- matches omukyala (woman).
abato = young/small. The prefix aba- matches abaana (children — plural).
ennungi = good. The prefix en- matches emmere (food).

word_classes.important_note: In Luganda, adjectives MUST agree with the noun they describe. The adjective changes its prefix to match the noun class. For example: omusajja omunene (big man) but abaana abato (young children) — the adjective prefix changes from omu- to aba- because the noun changed from singular to plural. This agreement system is a critical feature of Luganda grammar.

agreement_pattern.description: The adjective prefix mirrors the noun prefix
agreement_pattern.note: Full noun class agreement tables will be added in a future grammar file.

word_classes.description: Words that add more meaning to a verb — they tell you how, when, where, or how much an action happens.
word_classes.how_to_identify: If the word modifies a verb and answers 'how?', 'when?', 'where?', or 'how much?', it is an adverb.

examples.english: fell down hard / fell down very badly
examples.note: wansi = down/below. Nnyo = very much/hard. Together they intensify the verb.
mangu = quickly / soon. Modifies the verb 'to enter'.
mpola = slowly / gently. Modifies the verb 'to walk'.
nnyo ddala = very much indeed. Double intensifier — both words are adverbs.

sentence_examples.english: One who moves slowly reaches far.

word_classes.description: Joining words — words that connect two words, phrases, or sentences together.
word_classes.how_to_identify: If the word connects parts of a sentence, it is a conjunction.

examples.note: The most common conjunction. Used to join nouns and actions.
Important: 'nga' means 'like', 'as', or 'while' — not simply 'and'. Example: alya nga ayimiridde = eating while standing.
Used for choices and questions. Example: oba kituufu = whether it is true.
Used to connect two contrasting or sequential actions.
Used to give a reason. Example: akaaba kubanga bagenda = crying because they were going.
Used for contrast.

sentence_examples.english: When the woman was angry, the man made her laugh.

sentence_examples.luganda: Omwana akaaba kubanga maama ne Jane bagenda.
sentence_examples.english: The child was crying because mother and Jane were going.

word_classes.luganda_name: Akagambo akalaga Ekifo, Embera, Ekiseera oba Engeri
word_classes.description: Words or groups of words that come before a noun or pronoun to show place, position, or time.
word_classes.how_to_identify: If the word tells you where something is, when something happens, or how things relate in space or time, it is a preposition.

examples.note: ku mulimu = at work. ku ssaawa = at the hour.
mu nnyumba = inside the house. mu kitongole = in the department.
e Kampala = in Kampala.
wansi w'ekigere = under the foot.
waggulu w'olugya = above the compound.
kumpi ne = near to.
ewa Musa = at Moses's place.

sentence_examples.english: I work in the agriculture department.
I go to work every day.
Today she is going to hospital.

word_classes.description: Words or phrases used to show surprise, wonder, excitement, or strong emotion. They often stand alone and are not grammatically connected to the rest of the sentence.
word_classes.how_to_identify: If the word expresses sudden emotion, surprise, or a strong social signal — and could stand alone — it is an interjection.

question_words.description: Common question words used to form questions in Luganda. These often appear at the end of a Luganda sentence, unlike English where they appear at the start.

entries.example: Lwaki ogenda? = Why are you going?
Okomawo ddi? = When will you return?
Ogenda wa? = Where are you going?
Ekkubo ki? = Which road? Kiki oyo? = What is that?
Ani akulira wano? = Who is in charge here?
Gyani? = Whose is it? (form changes with noun class)
Mmeka? = How much does it cost? How many are there?
Muli mutya? = How are you (plural)? Otya? = How are you (singular)?
